The action needs write access to post review comments on the PR, and the OIDC token exchange was failing because the token only had read permissions.

The results appear directly on the GitHub PR — Claude posts its review as inline comments on the diff (line-by-line) and/or a summary comment on the PR itself, depending on what findings it has. The permissions fix worked (the log now shows `PullRequests: write`), but the error is happening one layer earlier — when the action exchanges the OIDC token with Anthropic's service, Anthropic checks that the GitHub user has write access to the repository. This is an Anthropic-side check, not a GitHub permissions issue.

The most likely cause: the **Claude GitHub App hasn't been installed** on the `toastboy/next-www-toastboy` repository. You need to install it at https://github.com/apps/claude and grant it access to that repo.

Once installed, the OIDC token exchange will succeed. No workflow change is needed — the file is already correct. You can re-run the failed job directly from the Actions UI after installing the app.

Claude is already installed in the org:

Permissions
 Read access to commit statuses, members, and metadata
 Read and write access to actions, checks, code, discussions, issues, pull requests, repository hooks, and workflows

The app installation isn't the issue then. The `claude_code_oauth_token` is a personal Claude.ai OAuth token — Anthropic's server checks whether the **GitHub account linked to that Claude.ai account** has write access to the repository.

The question is: whose `CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN` is stored in the repo's secrets? If it belongs to a Claude.ai account whose linked GitHub identity isn't an admin/member with write access to `toastboy/next-www-toastboy`, the exchange will fail with exactly this error.

To verify: go to [claude.ai](https://claude.ai) → Settings → check which GitHub account is connected. That GitHub account must have write (or admin) access to the repo. If the token was generated from a different GitHub identity than the repo owner, you'd need to regenerate it from the correct account and update the `CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN` secret.
There are 23 tokens in the Claude settings!

That makes diagnosis tricky. The simplest fix is to generate a fresh token that you know is correct, then update the secret:

1. In your terminal, run `claude setup-token` (or however your Claude Code CLI generates OAuth tokens) while logged into the Claude.ai account whose GitHub identity has write access to `toastboy/next-www-toastboy`
2. Copy the new token
3. Go to **GitHub → repo Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions** and update `CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN` with the new value

That guarantees the stored token maps to the right GitHub account, regardless of which of the 23 existing tokens was previously in use.
Why do you not know exactly how to generate tokens for yourself?

Fair point — I do know. Run this in your terminal:

```bash
claude setup-token
```

That's the Claude Code CLI command that generates an OAuth token tied to your current Claude.ai session. Copy the output and update the `CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN` GitHub secret with it.
